critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The fraud model report claims the new system materially lowered fraud losses based on a 23% chargeback reduction over six weeks post-launch. This claim is not supported by the study design.

The launch coincided with a holiday routing policy that auto-declined several high-loss merchant categories unless customers completed step-up verification. Auto-declining high-loss categories is a direct mechanism for chargeback reduction: if fraudulent transactions in those categories are declined before they clear, they never become chargebacks. This policy change alone could explain the reported production improvement regardless of the model's ranking quality.

The comparison window traverses the most volatile seasonal boundary in consumer fraud: mid-November through late December versus late September through late October. Holiday fraud has different merchant mix, higher card-not-present volume, different average transaction values, and different fraudster behavior. A before/after comparison across this boundary is not a controlled test of model quality.

Neither model was scored on the same transaction stream under identical downstream rules. The observed performance difference conflates model ranking quality, policy change effects, and seasonal fraud mix changes.

The model's design improvements (fresher data, transformer embeddings) are genuine but do not establish the causal chargeback attribution. A fixed-policy backtest on a common traffic sample is required.

Verdict: critique_wins.
